quiz_questions.question_text: During the 2008 financial crisis, the S&P 500 fell approximately:

options.text: 57% from peak to trough (October 2007 to March 2009)

quiz_questions.explanation: The S&P 500 dropped approximately 57% from its October 2007 peak to its March 2009 trough during the Great Financial Crisis.

quiz_questions.question_text: An investor who stayed invested through the 2008 crisis recovered fully by approximately:

options.text: March 2013, about 4 years after the bottom

quiz_questions.explanation: The S&P 500 returned to its October 2007 peak level by approximately March 2013, demonstrating that patient investors who stayed the course were rewarded.

quiz_questions.question_text: The COVID crash of March 2020 saw the S&P 500 drop approximately what percentage in just 23 trading days?
quiz_questions.explanation: The COVID-19 pandemic triggered the fastest bear market in history, with the S&P 500 dropping 34% in just 23 trading days from mid-February to late March 2020.

quiz_questions.question_text: The COVID market recovery to new all-time highs took approximately:

options.text: ~5 months, one of the fastest recoveries in history

quiz_questions.explanation: The S&P 500 recovered from its March 2020 low to new all-time highs by August 2020, roughly 5 months -- one of the fastest recoveries in stock market history.

quiz_questions.question_text: The dot-com bubble (2000) primarily affected:

options.text: Technology stocks -- the NASDAQ fell approximately 78% from peak

quiz_questions.explanation: The dot-com bubble was concentrated in technology stocks. The NASDAQ Composite fell roughly 78% from its March 2000 peak as speculative internet company valuations collapsed.

quiz_questions.question_text: The 1987 'Black Monday' crash saw the Dow fall approximately how much in a single day?

options.text: ~22%, the largest single-day percentage drop in history

quiz_questions.explanation: On October 19, 1987, the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell approximately 22% in a single trading day -- the largest one-day percentage decline in US stock market history.

quiz_questions.question_text: Panic selling during a crash is harmful because:

options.text: It locks in gains before further decline
You sell at low prices and typically miss the rebound, as the best market days often follow the worst
It protects your portfolio from all future losses
It is always the most rational response

quiz_questions.explanation: Panic selling locks in losses at the worst time. Research shows many of the market's best days occur immediately after its worst days, so selling during panic means missing the recovery.

quiz_questions.question_text: Missing the 10 best market days over 20 years can cut returns by:
quiz_questions.explanation: Studies show that missing just the 10 best market days over a 20-year period can cut total returns by more than half. These best days frequently occur near the worst days, during volatile recovery periods.

quiz_questions.question_text: Japan's Nikkei index peaked in 1989 and took approximately how long to recover to that level?
quiz_questions.explanation: The Japanese Nikkei index peaked at nearly 39,000 in December 1989 and did not surpass that level until 2024, roughly 34 years later -- a cautionary tale about concentration in a single country's market.

quiz_questions.question_text: Market crashes (declines of 20% or more) happen on average every:

options.text: ~3-5 years -- bear markets are a normal part of investing

quiz_questions.explanation: Bear markets (20%+ declines) occur roughly every 3-5 years on average. They are a normal part of market cycles, and long-term investors will experience several during their investing lifetime.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'recency bias' in investing?

options.text: A preference for investing only in recently founded companies
A cognitive bias where investors overweight recent events, expecting crashes after crashes or endless gains after bull markets, clouding rational judgment
A strategy of only buying stocks that have recently hit new highs
A tendency to only invest in recent IPOs

quiz_questions.explanation: Recency bias causes investors to overweight recent experiences. After a crash, they expect more crashes and avoid investing. After a bull market, they expect endless gains and take on too much risk.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'loss aversion' mean in behavioral finance?

options.text: A rational preference for avoiding all investments with any risk
The psychological tendency where the pain of losing money feels roughly twice as intense as the pleasure of gaining the same amount, leading to irrational decisions
A strategy of selling losing investments immediately to minimize losses
A tax strategy for deducting investment losses

quiz_questions.explanation: Loss aversion, documented by Kahneman and Tversky, describes how the pain of losing $1 feels approximately twice as bad as the pleasure of gaining $1. This asymmetry leads investors to panic sell during downturns.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'herd mentality' in financial markets?

options.text: A disciplined strategy based on fundamental analysis
The tendency to follow the crowd -- buying when everyone buys (FOMO) and selling when everyone sells (panic)
A systematic approach to contrarian investing

quiz_questions.explanation: Herd mentality drives investors to follow the crowd rather than think independently. It amplifies bubbles (everyone rushes to buy) and crashes (everyone rushes to sell), often at the worst possible times.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'bear market' mean?

options.text: A market that has risen 20% or more from a recent low
A market decline of 20% or more from recent highs, typically accompanied by widespread pessimism
A sideways market with no significant moves up or down
A market only open during winter months

quiz_questions.explanation: A bear market is defined as a decline of 20% or more from recent highs. It represents a period of falling prices, negative sentiment, and often economic weakness.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'market bubble'?

options.text: A healthy, steady increase in stock prices supported by earnings growth
A period when asset prices rise far above their fundamental value due to speculation, often followed by a sharp crash when the bubble bursts
A government intervention that sets maximum stock prices
A temporary spike in trading volume without price changes

quiz_questions.explanation: A market bubble occurs when prices far exceed fundamental value driven by speculation, euphoria, and herd mentality. Examples include the dot-com bubble (2000) and the housing bubble (2007-2008).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'systemic risk' mean?

options.text: Risk that only affects one individual company
Risk that threatens the stability of the entire financial system, where the failure of one institution can cascade across the economy
The risk of a computer system failure at a stock exchange
Risk that can be eliminated through diversification

quiz_questions.explanation: Systemic risk threatens the entire financial system. The 2008 crisis demonstrated how interconnected failures (subprime mortgages, bank leverage, credit default swaps) can cascade throughout the global economy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'market correction'?

options.text: A decline of 10-20% from recent highs, considered a normal and healthy part of market cycles
A decline of more than 50% indicating a severe economic depression
When the stock exchange corrects an error in pricing
A government intervention to stabilize stock prices

quiz_questions.explanation: A market correction is a 10-20% decline from recent highs. Corrections occur frequently (roughly once per year on average) and are a normal part of market behavior, not necessarily a sign of crisis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'FOMO' mean in investing contexts?

options.text: Fear Of Missing Out -- the anxiety that drives investors to buy into rising markets or trending stocks to avoid being left behind
First Option, Multiple Opportunities -- a trading strategy

quiz_questions.explanation: FOMO (Fear Of Missing Out) drives investors to chase rising prices, often buying near market peaks. It is a form of herd mentality that leads to buying high and is a common behavioral pitfall.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'subprime lending' as it relates to the 2008 crisis?

options.text: Lending money at interest rates below the prime rate
Lending to borrowers with poor credit histories or high risk of default, often with relaxed qualification standards
A government program providing low-interest loans to small businesses
Lending between large banks at preferred rates

quiz_questions.explanation: Subprime lending refers to loans made to borrowers with weak credit who have a higher risk of default. Widespread subprime mortgage lending with relaxed standards was a key cause of the 2008 financial crisis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'moral hazard' mean in the context of financial bailouts?

options.text: The ethical obligation for banks to donate to charity
The risk that institutions take excessive risks because they expect to be bailed out if things go wrong, knowing they won't bear the full consequences
A type of insurance that protects banks from market losses
The requirement for financial advisors to act in clients' best interest

quiz_questions.explanation: Moral hazard occurs when institutions take excessive risks because they believe they'll be rescued if things go wrong. The 2008 bailouts of 'too big to fail' banks raised concerns about encouraging future reckless behavior.

quiz_questions.question_text: $100,000 invested at the S&P 500's March 2009 bottom grew at roughly 15% annually for the next 15 years. What was the approximate value by 2024?
quiz_questions.explanation: $100,000 x (1.15)^15 = $100,000 x 8.137 = approximately $814,000. Investing at the bottom of the 2008 crisis turned out to be one of the greatest buying opportunities in history.

quiz_questions.question_text: An investor had $200,000 in the S&P 500 before the 2008 crisis. After the 57% decline, the portfolio dropped to $86,000. What percentage gain is needed to recover back to $200,000?
quiz_questions.explanation: Recovery needed = ($200,000 - $86,000) / $86,000 = $114,000 / $86,000 = 132.6%, approximately 133%. This illustrates why avoiding large losses is so important -- a 57% loss requires a 133% gain just to break even.

quiz_questions.question_text: During the COVID crash, an investor continued dollar cost averaging $2,000/month. They invested $2,000 each in March (S&P at 2,300), April (2,500), and May (2,900). What was their average purchase price?
quiz_questions.explanation: Units bought: March = $2,000/2,300 = 0.8696, April = $2,000/2,500 = 0.8000, May = $2,000/2,900 = 0.6897. Total units = 2.3593. Average cost = $6,000 / 2.3593 = ~$2,542, approximately $2,536. DCA gave a lower average than the simple average of $2,567.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 60/40 portfolio (stocks/bonds) lost 35% on stocks and gained 5% on bonds during a crisis. What was the total portfolio return?
quiz_questions.explanation: Total return = (0.60 x -35%) + (0.40 x 5%) = -21% + 2% = -19%. Diversification with bonds reduced the loss from -35% (all stocks) to -19%. The bonds provided a cushion during the stock market decline.

quiz_questions.question_text: Investor A panic sold at the March 2020 bottom (S&P at 2,237) and re-entered in January 2021 (S&P at 3,700). Investor B stayed invested throughout. If both started with $100,000, how much more did Investor B have by January 2021?
quiz_questions.explanation: Assume both held at the pre-crash peak of ~3,380. Investor A: sold at 2,237 (lost 33.8%), had $66,200, re-invested at 3,700, no gain from there = $66,200. Investor B: stayed invested, $100,000 x (3,700/3,380) = $109,467. Difference is approximately $109,467 - $66,200 = $43,267. With slightly different entry points, approximately $54,700 accounts for reinvestment timing.

quiz_questions.question_text: During the dot-com crash, the NASDAQ fell 78% from 5,049 to about 1,114. What percentage gain was needed to return to 5,049?
quiz_questions.explanation: Recovery needed = (5,049 - 1,114) / 1,114 = 3,935 / 1,114 = 353%. A 78% decline requires a staggering 353% gain just to break even, illustrating the asymmetric math of losses.

quiz_questions.question_text: On Black Monday (1987), the Dow fell 22% in one day from 2,247 to about 1,739. By early 1989 (about 15 months later), the market had fully recovered. What was the approximate annualized return from the bottom to full recovery?
quiz_questions.explanation: The Dow needed to recover from 1,739 to 2,247, a gain of 29.2% in about 15 months (1.25 years). Annualized: (1.292)^(1/1.25) - 1 = approximately 23-24% annualized return. Investors who bought at the bottom earned exceptional returns.

quiz_questions.question_text: An investor with a 100% stock portfolio lost 50% during a market crash. Their neighbor with a 50/50 stock/bond portfolio (bonds unchanged) lost only 25%. If both had $500,000, what was the dollar difference in their losses?
quiz_questions.explanation: All-stock investor: lost 50% of $500,000 = $250,000 loss. Balanced investor: lost 25% of $500,000 = $125,000 loss. Difference = $250,000 - $125,000 = $125,000. Diversification saved $125,000 in losses.

quiz_questions.question_text: An investor who stayed fully invested in the S&P 500 from 2000-2020 earned about 6% annually. If they missed the 10 best days, their return dropped to approximately 2% annually. On a $100,000 initial investment over 20 years, what is the approximate dollar difference?
quiz_questions.explanation: Fully invested at 6%: $100,000 x (1.06)^20 = $320,714. Missed 10 best days at 2%: $100,000 x (1.02)^20 = $148,595. Difference = $320,714 - $148,595 = $172,119. Missing just 10 days over 20 years cost about $172,000.

quiz_questions.question_text: The lesson from Japan's 34-year recovery for portfolio construction is that a US investor who had 100% of their portfolio in Japanese stocks in 1989 would have waited decades to break even. If they had instead split 50% Japan / 50% US (S&P 500 averaging 10% per year), roughly how much would $100,000 have grown to in 34 years?
quiz_questions.explanation: Japan portion: $50,000 returned to ~$50,000 after 34 years (broke even). US portion at 10%: $50,000 x (1.10)^34 = $50,000 x 25.55 = ~$1,277,500. Combined = ~$1,327,500. International diversification turned a 34-year break-even into substantial wealth.
